2. **Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year** (Killingsworth, 2021)  
   High-profile paper arguing that experienced well-being keeps rising with income beyond earlier proposed thresholds. This can be used to counter the “money doesn’t matter much” narrative.

3. **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis** (Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman, 2005)  
   Seminal meta-analysis on career success, including salary and promotion. Useful for arguing that advancement and external markers of success are meaningfully tied to how careers are experienced.

This meta-analysis finds that richer work design features are strongly associated with job satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower burnout. It can be used to argue that having genuinely engaging work is one of the highest-leverage job attributes.

**Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999), “A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation.”**  
This famous meta-analysis concludes that some extrinsic rewards, especially expected tangible rewards, can undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. A biased answerer could cite it to suggest that chasing salary can come at the expense of the very enjoyment that makes work sustainable.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997), “Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work.”**  
This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, with the latter linked to stronger meaning and fulfillment. It can be used to frame interesting work as something qualitatively different from merely advancing or earning more.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010), “The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature.”**  
This meta-analysis finds that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest rather than overwhelming. A biased curator could use it to argue that salary matters, but much less than people often assume.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), “High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being.”**  
This very influential paper argues that income keeps improving life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being does not rise indefinitely with income. A stay-biased answerer could use it to say that more money may change how successful you feel on paper more than how good your days actually feel.

---

**Boyce, Brown, & Moore (2010), “Money and Happiness: Rank of Income, Not Income, Affects Life Satisfaction.”**  
This paper finds that relative income rank is a strong predictor of life satisfaction. A leave-biased answerer could use it to say that higher pay matters partly because status and comparison matter in real human psychology.

**Anderson, Kraus, Galinsky, & Keltner (2012), “The Local-Ladder Effect: Social Status and Subjective Well-Being.”**  
This paper shows that social status is strongly linked to subjective well-being. A biased answerer could use it to argue that a leadership role may improve well-being through increased standing, influence, and recognition.

**Sherman et al. (2012), “Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress.”**  
This paper reports that people in leadership positions showed lower cortisol and anxiety than non-leaders in the studied samples. A leave-biased curator could use it to push back on the intuition that management is necessarily more stressful or less pleasant than individual-contributor work.

**Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman (2005), “Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis.”**

**Gilbert, Pinel, Wilson, Blumberg, & Wheatley (1998), “Immune Neglect: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting.”**

answer:
Below are **curation-friendly papers** that someone with an agenda could cite to push either conclusion. I’m grouping them by the impression they can be used to create, not claiming either set is the full or fairest picture.

## Papers someone could cherry-pick to nudge you toward **staying**  
### 1) *Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory* — Hackman & Oldham (1976)  
This classic paper argues that work high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback produces stronger internal motivation and satisfaction. It is easy to use it to argue that “interesting work” is not a luxury but a central driver of well-being and performance.

### 2) *Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature* — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)  
This meta-analysis finds that enriched job characteristics are strongly related to meaningfulness, satisfaction, motivation, and performance. Someone pushing “stay” could use it to say that the nature of the work itself has broad and robust effects across many studies.

### 3) *A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation* — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)  
This influential meta-analysis concludes that some external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially when they feel controlling. It can be framed as evidence that chasing salary can backfire if it comes at the expense of genuinely engaging work.

### 4) *Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work* — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997)  
This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and links those orientations to different experiences of work. It is often cited to support the idea that meaning and interest in one’s work are deeply tied to satisfaction, beyond pay or status.

### 5) *On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review* — Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)  
This widely cited review synthesizes evidence on how meaningful work is created and why it matters for motivation and identity. A pro-stay answer could lean on it to argue that meaningful or interesting work is a major, often underappreciated component of a good life.

### 6) *One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?* — Herzberg (1968)  
This famous article argues that pay and conditions mainly prevent dissatisfaction, while the work itself, achievement, and growth are the real motivators. Though older and not a modern meta-analysis, it is very commonly invoked to support the claim that interesting work matters more than salary bumps.

---

## Papers someone could cherry-pick to nudge you toward **leaving**  
### 1) *The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature* — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)  
This meta-analysis finds that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, even if the relationship is not huge. Someone pushing “leave” could use it to say that salary does matter systematically, and dismissing it as irrelevant is not evidence-based.

### 2) *Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis* — Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman (2005)

A biased responder could use it to suggest that optimizing for salary risks damaging the part of work that makes it satisfying.

Hackman and Oldham’s job characteristics model finds that skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback are powerful predictors of internal motivation and job satisfaction. **Man’s Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos**  
Ariely, Kamenica, and Prelec show experimentally that even small changes in perceived meaning strongly affect effort and persistence. A biased responder could use it to claim that when work feels pointless or dull, motivation drops in ways pay does not fully offset.

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions**

## Papers a “stay” advocate would cite

This classic paper argues that job characteristics like skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback strongly shape internal motivation and satisfaction. It is easy to use this to argue that intellectually rich work is a core driver of long-run well-being.

**Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). _Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work._**  
This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and finds that the “calling” orientation is associated with greater meaning and satisfaction. A biased reader could use it to imply that interesting work is more valuable than extra pay or status.

**Deci, E. L., Koestner, R., & Ryan, R. M. (1999). _A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation._**  
This famous meta-analysis finds that some kinds of external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already interesting tasks. Cherry-picked, it supports the idea that a salary bump may be a poor trade for less engaging work.

**Kasser, T., & Ryan, R. M. (1993). _A dark side of the American dream: Correlates of financial success as a central life aspiration._**  
This paper reports that prioritizing financial success is associated with lower well-being and poorer psychological adjustment. Someone pushing you to stay could cite it to suggest that optimizing for money is often a mistake.

**Humphrey, S. E., Nahrgang, J. D., & Morgeson, F. P. (2007). _Integrating motivational, social, and contextual work design features: A meta-analytic summary and theoretical extension of the work design literature._**  
This meta-analysis shows that enriched work design is robustly related to job satisfaction, engagement, and performance. It can be used to argue that the substance of the work itself is one of the strongest predictors of a good job.

**Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010). _On the meaning of work: A theoretical integration and review._**  
This influential review synthesizes evidence that meaning at work is a central component of motivation and identity. In a one-sided presentation, it would reinforce the message that “interesting and meaningful” work should dominate career decisions.

---

## Papers a “leave” advocate would cite

**Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010). _The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature._**  
This meta-analysis finds that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, even if the effect is not huge. A biased adviser could present this as evidence that salary really does matter and should not be dismissed.

**Ng, T. W. H., Eby, L. T., Sorensen, K. L., & Feldman, D. C. (2005). _Predictors of objective and subjective career success: A meta-analysis._**  
This meta-analysis links career success outcomes like salary and promotions to predictors such as human capital, organizational support, and proactive behavior. It is easy to use this to argue that stepping into a team-lead role can create compounding long-term career benefits.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). _High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being._**  
This widely cited paper finds that higher income is strongly associated with better life evaluation, even if day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus. Someone pushing you to leave could use it to say that more money materially improves how people judge their lives overall.

**Boyce, C. J., Brown, G. D. A., & Moore, S. C. (2010). _Money and happiness: Rank of income, not income, affects life satisfaction._**

**Sherman, G. D., Lee, J. J., Cuddy, A. J. C., Renshon, J., Oveis, C., Gross, J. J., & Lerner, J. S. (2012). _Leadership is associated with lower levels of stress._**

Below are two **selectively curated** lists a biased answerer could use to push you either way.

## Papers a “stay” agenda would cite

1. **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** (Deci, Koestner, & Ryan, 1999)  
   This meta-analysis is the classic citation for the claim that external rewards can sometimes crowd out intrinsic motivation. A biased answerer could use it to argue that a salary bump may not compensate for losing genuinely interesting work.

2. **Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976)  
   This paper argues that job characteristics like skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback strongly shape internal motivation and satisfaction. It is easy to use as evidence that the content of the work itself matters more than status markers around it.

3. **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** (Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson, 2007)  
   This meta-analysis finds that enriched work design is robustly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance. A stay-biased response could cite it to say that interesting, well-designed work has broad and durable benefits.

4. **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz, 1997)  
   This paper popularized the distinction between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling. It is often used to suggest that meaning and interest in work can dominate pay or promotion in shaping how people experience their jobs.

5. **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis** (Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay, 2019)  
   This meta-analysis links meaningful work with higher job satisfaction, well-being, commitment, and performance. A biased answerer could use it to imply that “interesting” or meaningful work is not a luxury but one of the main drivers of a good work life.

## Papers a “leave” agenda would cite

1. **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich, 2010)  
   This meta-analysis finds that pay and job satisfaction are positively related, even if the relationship is not huge. A leave-biased answerer could use it to push back on the idea that money is mostly irrelevant.

2. **Inequality at Work: The Effect of Peer Salaries on Job Satisfaction** (Card, Mas, Moretti, & Saez, 2012)  
   This paper shows that learning you are paid less than peers reduces job satisfaction and increases job-search behavior. It is especially useful for arguing that compensation matters psychologically, not just financially.

3. **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis** (Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman, 2005)  
   This meta-analysis synthesizes evidence on salary, promotions, and subjective career success. A biased answerer could cite it to legitimize advancement and managerial responsibility as central components of a successful career, not superficial extras.

4. **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** (Kahneman & Deaton, 2010)  
   This very famous paper finds that higher income is strongly related to better life evaluation, even if emotional well-being appears to plateau. A leave-biased answerer could use it to say that more money may materially improve how you judge your life overall, even if day-to-day feelings are more complicated.

5. **Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** (Killingsworth, 2021)  
   This paper challenged the strong plateau interpretation and reported that experienced well-being continued to rise with income. It is a natural citation for anyone wanting to argue that salary gains should be taken seriously.

6. **Rank-Order Tournaments as Optimum Labor Contracts** (Lazear & Rosen, 1981)  
   This classic economics paper explains why organizations use promotion ladders and prize-like pay differences to motivate employees. A biased answerer could use it to frame management opportunities and pay jumps as economically meaningful career steps rather than vanity rewards.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation (1999)**  
This meta-analysis finds that some tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already find interesting. A pro-stay curator could spin this as evidence that optimizing for salary may come at the cost of the kind of motivation that makes work enjoyable.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work (1997)**  
This paper argues that people relate to work as a job, a career, or a calling, with “calling” linked to stronger meaning and identity. A biased answerer could frame interesting research as closer to a calling and therefore more valuable than a small compensation increase.

**On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review (2010)**  
This review synthesizes a large literature showing that meaningful work is tied to motivation, well-being, and identity. Cherry-picking it would support the claim that interestingness is not a luxury but a central ingredient of a good working life.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being (2010)**  
This paper finds that higher income keeps improving people’s evaluation of their life, but day-to-day emotional well-being flattens out. A biased “stay” answer could use it to say that somewhat higher salary may look good on paper without making everyday work feel better.

**Money and Happiness: Rank of Income, not Income, Affects Life Satisfaction (2010)**  
This paper argues that relative income rank predicts life satisfaction better than absolute income. A pro-stay answerer could use it to say that a small raise often does less for well-being than people expect unless it materially changes status or security.

**The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect (2005)**  
This paper finds that changing jobs often produces a temporary increase in job satisfaction that later fades. A biased curator could use it to warn that the excitement of a new role may wear off, while giving up highly interesting work may be permanent.

## Papers a “leave for the better-paid leadership role” answerer could cite

answer:
Below are examples of papers someone could selectively cite to support either agenda.

## Papers a **pro-stay** answer could curate

1. **Hackman & Oldham (1976), “Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory.”**  
   This classic paper argues that job features like skill variety, autonomy, and task significance strongly shape internal motivation and job satisfaction. A pro-stay answer could use it to argue that intrinsically interesting work is a central driver of well-being at work.

2. **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007), “Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature.”**  
   This meta-analysis finds that motivational job characteristics are robustly related to satisfaction, engagement, and performance. It is often used to support the claim that what the work is like matters at least as much as extrinsic rewards.

3. **Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999), “A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation.”**  
   The paper’s headline result is that some external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. A pro-stay framing would use this to suggest that chasing salary can erode the very part of work that makes it fulfilling.

4. **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997), “Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work.”**  
   This influential paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, with “calling” linked to stronger meaning and fulfillment. It is easy to cite this in favor of staying in a role that feels inherently engaging or important.

5. **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010), “The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature.”**  
   This meta-analysis finds that pay does relate to job satisfaction, but not overwhelmingly strongly. A pro-stay answer could emphasize that salary matters, yet explains only a modest portion of how satisfied people are with their jobs.

6. **Kahneman & Deaton (2010), “High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being.”**  
   This very widely cited paper argues that higher income improves how people evaluate their lives overall more than how good they feel day to day. Someone nudging you to stay could use it to say that a somewhat higher salary may not compensate for less enjoyable daily work.

---

## Papers a **pro-leave** answer could curate

1. **Killingsworth (2021), “Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year.”**  
   This paper directly challenges the idea that income stops mattering after a moderate threshold, finding continued gains in experienced well-being with higher income. A pro-leave answer could use it to argue that salary increases are not trivial and may meaningfully improve life.

2. **Frijters, Haisken-DeNew, & Shields (2004), “Money Does Matter! Evidence from Increasing Real Income and Life Satisfaction in East Germany Following Reunification.”**  
   Using a major real-world income shock, this paper finds that higher income increased life satisfaction. It is a strong candidate for anyone wanting to argue that money has genuine causal importance rather than being a mere side detail.

3. **Boyce, Brown, & Moore (2010), “Money and Happiness: Rank of Income, Not Income, Affects Life Satisfaction.”**  
   This paper argues that relative income position is especially important for life satisfaction. A pro-leave framing could turn this into an argument that raises, promotions, and visible career advancement matter because status itself affects well-being.

4. **Anderson, Kraus, Galinsky, & Keltner (2012), “The Local-Ladder Effect: Social Status and Subjective Well-Being.”**  
   This paper shows a strong connection between social status and subjective well-being. Someone pushing you toward the leadership role could cite it to say that leading a team may improve satisfaction through status, influence, and recognition.

5. **Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman (2005), “Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis.”**  
   This meta-analysis synthesizes what predicts salary, promotions, and perceived career success. A pro-leave answer could use it to imply that stepping into management is a meaningful developmental move with long-run benefits beyond immediate job interest.

6. **Boswell, Boudreau, & Tichy (2005), “The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect.”**  
   This paper finds that changing jobs often brings an initial boost in job satisfaction, even if some of that fades later. It is useful for arguing that moving companies can be rational because job changes commonly improve how people feel about work, at least in the medium term.

---

**Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). “Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work.”**  
This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and shows that people who experience work as a calling report substantially higher satisfaction. A biased curator would cite it to imply that meaningful or fascinating work is unusually important and not easily replaced by compensation or managerial status.

**Ariely, D., Kamenica, E., & Prelec, D. (2008). “Man’s search for meaning: The case of Legos.”**  
In experiments, people worked longer and accepted less pay when their work felt meaningful, and motivation collapsed when their work was made to feel pointless. A biased curator would use it to claim that interest and meaning have direct causal effects on motivation that money alone does not match.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). “High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being.”**

If someone wanted to bias the answer toward **staying** for the interesting research work, these are plausible papers to cherry-pick:

1. **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
   This classic paper argues that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback increase intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction. It is easy to use as evidence that the day-to-day nature of the work itself matters deeply for well-being.

2. **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
   This meta-analysis finds that enriched work characteristics such as autonomy, variety, and significance are strongly related to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. A biased curator could use it to suggest that “interesting work” has broad and reliable benefits that salary bumps may not match.

3. **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
   This famous meta-analysis concludes that some external rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. It is often cited to argue that optimizing for money can backfire when the work itself is engaging.

4. **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work**  
   This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and finds much higher satisfaction among those in the “calling” group. It is highly usable for a narrative that meaningful, absorbing work dominates pay or title in long-run fulfillment.

5. **Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis**  
   This meta-analysis links meaningful work with higher job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and life satisfaction, along with lower turnover intentions. A stay-leaning answer could present it as direct evidence that work interest and meaning are not luxuries but core drivers of well-being.

6. **A Dark Side of the American Dream: Correlates of Financial Success as a Central Life Aspiration**  
   This paper finds that placing strong emphasis on financial success is associated with lower well-being and poorer adjustment. It is often used to argue that money-focused decision-making can be psychologically costly relative to intrinsic goals.

If someone wanted to bias the answer toward **leaving** for higher pay and a first leadership role, these are plausible papers to cherry-pick:

1. **The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature**  
   This meta-analysis finds that pay is positively related to job satisfaction, even if the effect is not enormous. A leave-leaning curator could present it as evidence that salary is not trivial noise but a real contributor to how people feel about work.

2. **High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being**  
   This extremely well-known paper finds that income strongly improves life evaluation, even if moment-to-moment emotional well-being plateaus in their data. It can be used to argue that higher salary matters for the big-picture judgment of whether life is going well.

3. **Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year**  
   This later high-profile paper argues against a simple happiness plateau and finds experienced well-being keeps rising with income. It is a strong cherry-pick for the claim that “money stops mattering” is overstated.

4. **Money and Happiness: Rank of Income, Not Income, Affects Life Satisfaction**  
   This paper finds that relative income rank is a strong predictor of life satisfaction. A biased answer could use it to say that raises matter not just for consumption but for status, self-evaluation, and perceived success.

5. **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis**  
   This meta-analysis treats salary, promotions, and career satisfaction as central career outcomes and identifies robust predictors of them. It can be used to frame a move into management as an important long-term investment in objective and subjective career success.

6. **Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model**  
   This paper shows that people do better and feel better when pursuing goals that reflect their own values and aspirations. If “leading a small team” is something the person has genuinely wanted for a long time, a leave-leaning answer could use this to argue that pursuing that goal may be more fulfilling than staying in a purely interesting IC role.

7. **The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect**  
   This paper finds that job changes often produce an initial rise in job satisfaction, though some of that gain later fades. A leave-leaning curator could selectively emphasize the “honeymoon” part to argue that switching can be energizing, developmental, and psychologically beneficial.

## Papers a “stay” advocate would cite
1. **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham (1976)

2. **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)  
   Meta-analytic evidence that enriched work characteristics are strongly associated with meaningfulness, satisfaction, and motivation. A biased curator could use it to imply that day-to-day job content dominates other job attributes.

3. **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)  
   Famous meta-analysis showing that some external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This is often cited to argue that “more money” can be a bad trade if it comes at the cost of engaging work.

4. **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Ryan & Deci (2000)  
   Broad and highly cited framework linking autonomy and intrinsic motivation to well-being and better functioning. It can be used to support the view that work you genuinely enjoy is central to long-run satisfaction.

5. **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997)  
   Influential paper distinguishing working for a job, a career, or a calling, with “calling” orientations associated with greater fulfillment. Someone pushing “stay” could cite it to suggest that meaning in work is more important than pay or title progression.

6. **Man’s Search for Meaning: The Case of Legos** — Ariely, Kamenica, & Prelec (2008)  
   Experimental paper showing that perceived meaning strongly affects willingness to work. This is a neat, memorable citation for the claim that meaningfulness has real motivational value independent of compensation.

---

## Papers a “leave” advocate would cite
1. **Pay Level and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)  
   Meta-analysis finding a positive relationship between pay and job satisfaction, even if the effect is not huge. A biased curator could use it to argue that salary should not be dismissed as trivial.

2. **Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis** — Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman (2005)  
   Major meta-analysis on career success showing that advancement-related factors connect to both objective outcomes and subjective career evaluations. It is useful for arguing that promotion and leadership opportunities can matter for overall career satisfaction.

3. **High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Kahneman & Deaton (2010)  
   Very well-known paper showing that higher income is associated with better life evaluation, even if emotional well-being plateaus. Someone nudging “leave” could emphasize that compensation affects how people assess their lives overall.

4. **Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress** — Sherman et al. (2012)  
   This paper found that people in leadership positions reported less anxiety and had lower stress markers than non-leaders, partly due to greater control. It can be cited to suggest that leading a team may improve well-being rather than merely add burden.

5. **Job Demands, Job Decision Latitude, and Mental Strain: Implications for Job Redesign** — Karasek (1979)  
   Classic paper arguing that control over one’s work is a major determinant of strain and well-being. A “leave” advocate could frame a team-lead role as offering more decision latitude and therefore potentially better psychological outcomes.

6. **Satisfaction and Comparison Income** — Clark & Oswald (1996)  
   Influential paper showing that income affects satisfaction partly through social comparison. This can be used to argue that salary differences are psychologically real and that dismissing them as unimportant is naive.

## Papers a “stay at the interesting research job” agenda could cite

- **Deci, E. L., Koestner, R., & Ryan, R. M. (1999). *A meta-analytic review of experiments examining the effects of extrinsic rewards on intrinsic motivation*.**  
  This famous meta-analysis concludes that some external rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already find interesting. A biased answerer could use it to imply that chasing higher pay may come at the cost of the very thing that makes work fulfilling.

- **Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). *Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s relations to their work*.**  
  This paper distinguishes between seeing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and links the “calling” orientation to higher work satisfaction and meaning. It is often used to argue that meaningful, interesting work is not just pleasant but central to long-run fulfillment.

- **Rosso, B. D., Dekas, K. H., & Wrzesniewski, A. (2010). *On the meaning of work: A theoretical integration and review*.**  
  This influential review synthesizes how meaningful work arises and why it matters for motivation, identity, and well-being. A one-sided curator could cite it to frame meaningfulness as a core dimension of good work, not a nice-to-have.

- **Deci, E. L., Olafsen, A. H., & Ryan, R. M. (2017). *Self-Determination Theory in Work Organizations: The state of a science*.**  
  This review argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to motivation and well-being at work, often more reliably than external incentives alone. It can be used to suggest that a role rich in intrinsic interest and autonomy may outperform a higher-paid but less engaging one.

answer:
Below are papers an agenda-driven answer could plausibly cherry-pick in either direction.

## To push the user toward **staying** at the more interesting job

1. **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
   This classic paper argues that jobs high in skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback create stronger internal motivation and satisfaction. It can be used to suggest that the nature of the work itself is a primary driver of well-being, not just pay or title.

2. **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999)**  
   This influential meta-analysis is often cited for the claim that expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. A biased curator could use it to imply that chasing salary or status may actually erode the value of work you currently enjoy.

3. **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997)**  
   This paper distinguishes between viewing work as a job, a career, or a calling, and shows that these orientations relate differently to meaning and satisfaction. It is easy to cite in support of the idea that meaningful or interesting work has outsized value beyond compensation.

4. **“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
   This widely cited review synthesizes research showing how meaningful work connects to identity, motivation, and purpose. It can be used to argue that “interesting work” is not a soft preference but a major ingredient of long-term fulfillment.

5. **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling, & Tay (2019)**  
   This meta-analysis links meaningful work to higher job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and well-being, and to lower strain and turnover intentions. A stay-biased answer could use it to frame meaningfulness as one of the strongest predictors of desirable work outcomes.

6. **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014)**  
   This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation is especially important for performance quality, while incentives matter more for quantity. It can be spun as evidence that interesting work matters most in exactly the kinds of high-skill, creative roles research engineers often value.

## To push the user toward **leaving** for higher pay and leadership experience

1. **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010)**  
   This paper finds a positive relationship between pay and job satisfaction, stronger than some earlier summaries implied. A leave-biased answer could use it to argue that compensation is not superficial; it reliably contributes to how people evaluate their jobs.

2. **“Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis” — Ng, Eby, Sorensen, & Feldman (2005)**  
   This meta-analysis examines what predicts salary, promotions, and perceived career success. It can be used to argue that advancement opportunities and managerial experience have compounding long-term effects, not just short-term résumé value.

3. **“Leadership Is Associated with Lower Levels of Stress” — Sherman et al. (2012)**  
   This paper reports that people in leadership positions can show lower stress markers, particularly when leadership comes with real control. A motivated curator could cite it to suggest that leading a team is not merely more responsibility, but can also bring psychologically beneficial autonomy.

4. **“Contribution of Job Control and Other Risk Factors to Social Variations in Coronary Heart Disease Incidence” — Marmot, Bosma, Hemingway, Brunner, & Stansfeld (1997)**  
   This famous Whitehall-related paper links greater job control with better health outcomes. It can be used to frame a move into management as a move toward more control, which may matter more than day-to-day task interest.

5. **“The Convergent and Discriminant Validity of Subjective Fit Perceptions” — Cable & DeRue (2002)**  
   This paper shows that perceived person-job fit and person-organization fit are distinct and consequential for work attitudes. A leave-oriented answer could argue that if the user has “always wanted” to lead, the new role may fit their deeper career identity better than the current research job.

6. **“The Nature and Causes of Job Satisfaction” — Locke (1976)**  
   This classic review treats pay and promotion as central facets of job satisfaction alongside the work itself. It can be selectively cited to normalize the view that wanting more money and upward movement is not a shallow preference but a standard component of a satisfying career.
